unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will explore the natural world — the weather and the climate, the beach and the coast, forests, mountains, and even our planet Earth. You will learn to talk about places you have visited using a new tense called the present perfect (“I have explored a forest”). You will describe nature with colourful words, ask good questions, and learn how to care for the environment. By the end, you will be ready to go on a nature adventure of your own, just like Adam and Amal in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few nature words each day.
Work through the nine short lessons in the Grammar file. Do one at a time — never all at once!
Read the Story file, “From Coast to Forest”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four parts below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Children observing and caring for the natural environment

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The usual weather in a place over a long time.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The desert has a hot, dry climate.
practiceSentences: The desert has a hot, dry climate.
A rainy climate keeps the hills green all year.
Does the climate change as you climb a mountain?
The teacher said, "Compare the desert climate with the coast."
I like our warm climate for playing outside.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - l - i - m - a - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'climate', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What the sky and air are like today.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Today the weather is sunny and warm.
practiceSentences: Today the weather is sunny and warm.
Stormy weather kept the fishing boats at home.
Will the weather be dry for sports day?
Sami said, "I recorded the weather all week."
My favourite weather is cool wind after rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - e - a - t - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'weather', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: How hot or cold something is.
practiceSentences: The temperature is 30 degrees today.
The temperature rises quickly in the desert at noon.
Does the temperature fall on the mountain top?
Maya said, "The temperature reached forty degrees today."
I wrote the morning temperature on our class chart.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - m - p - e - r - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'temperature', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The light and warmth that come from the sun.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sunshine helped the flowers grow.
practiceSentences: The sunshine helped the flowers grow.
Bright sunshine turned the puddles into little mirrors.
Do tomatoes need sunshine every day to grow?
Theo said, "Sunshine after rain makes a rainbow."
I sat in the sunshine and read my book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - n - s - h - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sunshine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze)
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The water in the pond froze in the cold night.
practiceSentences: The water in the pond froze in the cold night.
The puddles froze into slippery glass overnight.
Do you know why the mountain lake froze?
Samira said, "My juice froze into a tasty ice lolly."
My fingers felt like they froze in the cold wind.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'froze', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very tall hill with steep sides.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We saw white snow on top of the mountain.
practiceSentences: We saw white snow on top of the mountain.
The climbers rested halfway up the steep mountain.
Which mountain is the highest in the world?
Adam said, "The mountain path is rocky and narrow."
I drew a purple mountain behind the village.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - o - u - n - t - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'mountain', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big area with many trees.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The deer ran through the green forest.
practiceSentences: The deer ran through the green forest.
Owls sleep in the shady forest all day.
What animals live deep in the forest?
Sami said, "The forest smells of rain and leaves."
I heard birdsong echo through the forest.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - o - r - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'forest', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Land with sand near the sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I built sandcastles on the beach.
practiceSentences: I built sandcastles on the beach.
Tiny crabs hide under stones on the beach.
Shall we collect shells at the beach tomorrow?
Maya said, "The beach sand was warm under my feet."
I flew my kite along the windy beach.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'beach', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The land next to the sea.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Fishermen live in a village near the coast.
practiceSentences: Fishermen live in a village near the coast.
The road follows the coast past little fishing villages.
Can you see dolphins from the coast here?
Theo said, "The coast is cooler than the city."
I watched boats sail along the coast at sunset.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'coast', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The land, water, animals, and plants around us.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We took a walk to enjoy nature.
practiceSentences: We took a walk to enjoy nature.
Nature gives us shade, fruit, and fresh air.
What sounds of nature can you hear right now?
Samira said, "Nature is full of clever patterns."
I keep a nature diary of birds I see.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'nature', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go and look at new places.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We will explore the forest path tomorrow.
practiceSentences: We will explore the forest path tomorrow.
The class will explore the rock pools on the trip.
Which path shall we explore first today?
Adam said, "Let us explore the old olive grove."
I explore my grandmother's garden every visit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - l - o - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'explore', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Power that helps things move or work.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use the sun for clean energy.
practiceSentences: We use the sun for clean energy.
The sun gives clean energy to the solar panels.
Where does a car get its energy from?
The teacher said, "Food gives your body energy."
I have lots of energy after a good sleep.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - e - r - g - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'energy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What everything is made of; it takes up space.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Water, rock, and air are all types of matter.
practiceSentences: Water, rock, and air are all types of matter.
Solid, liquid, and gas are states of matter.
Is air a kind of matter too?
Sami said, "Everything around us is made of matter."
I sorted pictures of matter into solids and liquids.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - t - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'matter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A hard, shiny material used to make things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The spoon is made of metal.
practiceSentences: The spoon is made of metal.
The gate is made of strong, shiny metal.
Why does metal feel cold in the morning?
Maya said, "A magnet sticks to some metal things."
I found an old metal coin in the sand.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'metal', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A big ball in space, like Earth or Mars.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Earth is the planet we live on.
practiceSentences: Earth is the planet we live on.
Earth is the only planet with big blue oceans.
Which planet is famous for its rings?
Theo said, "Mars is called the red planet."
I made a model planet out of clay.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - l - a - n - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'planet', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Nature and the Environment.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe places in nature: the beach, coast, forest, and mountain.
Talk about the weather, the climate, and the temperature.
Say what you have done using the present perfect: “I have seen the sea.”
Ask and answer questions like “Have you ever explored a forest?” — “Yes, I have.”
Use describing words to paint a clear picture: “a metal rock on the beach.”
Give instructions to care for the planet: “Protect nature.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read and enjoy the story “From Coast to Forest”.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Our Wonderful Nature
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Nature is all around us. The sunshine warms the land. Trees grow tall in the forest. Waves splash on the beach near the coast. High above, the mountain touches the clouds. All of this is our home — the planet Earth. We must take care of it.

readings.title: A Poem: “Nature Is Our Home”
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Nature is our home,
It gives us water, trees, and air.
The sun, the sea, the mountain tall —
Let's take care, and share it all.

readings.title: Getting Ready for the Trip
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Tomorrow we will explore the coast and the forest. Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see. And remember — protect the planet!”

readings.title: Have You Ever…?
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: “Have you ever explored a forest?” asked Adam.  “Yes, I have!” said one friend.  “No, I haven't,” said the other. “I have only seen pictures.”

readings.title: From Coast to Forest
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was the first week of term, and Teacher Sarah had a big surprise. “We are going on a nature trip!” she said. The whole class cheered.
“We will explore the coast, play on the beach, and hike a mountain trail,” she said. Then she smiled. “Have you ever explored a forest?”
“Yes, I have!” said Adam.
“No, I haven't,” said Amal. “I have only seen pictures.”
The next day, the class packed their bags. “Bring water, wear a hat, and write what you see,” said Teacher Sarah. “And remember — protect the planet. Take only pictures, leave only footprints.”
Their first stop was the beach. The sunshine was bright, and the weather was hot. They felt the soft, warm sand and saw tiny crabs running near the waves.
“This is the coast,” said the teacher. “Look at the water and the wide sky.”
Adam pointed to something shiny in the sand. “It's a rock — no, it's metal!”
“Touch it carefully,” said Teacher Sarah. “It may be sharp.” The children sat down and wrote a little poem together:
I see the waves,
I see the sand,
I hold a metal rock in my hand.
Later, they drove to the forest. The air felt cooler here. “What is the temperature?” asked the teacher.
“Maybe 22 degrees,” said Amal.
“That's right!” she said. “The climate is different here — more shade, and less wind.”
They walked along a path between the tall trees. “Have you ever been in a forest this big?” asked Adam.
“No, I haven't,” said Amal. “It feels like another world.”
Suddenly, they saw something in the dirt — white and soft, like snow. “Is that ice?” asked Adam.
“No, it is not snow,” said the teacher. “But once, water here froze during a very cold night.”
“Frozen water, here in Africa?” asked Amal, surprised.
“Yes,” the teacher smiled. “Even here, the weather can surprise us.”
They climbed higher and reached the mountain viewpoint. “Look down,” said Teacher Sarah. “You can see the beach and the forest from up here.” The wind blew gently across their faces.
“The air feels so fresh,” said Amal.
“That's natural energy,” said the teacher. “It comes from the sun and the wind.”
On the way back, they stopped near a pond. “What is all this white foam?” asked Adam.
“It is a type of matter,” said Teacher Sarah. “Foam, water, rock, and air — they are all matter, just in different forms.”
Back at school, the students shared what they had learned. “I wrote about the sunshine,” said Adam. “It helps the plants grow near the coast.”
“I wrote about the forest,” said Amal. “I have seen animals and trees I never knew before.”
The teacher asked, “Have you ever visited the beach or a mountain before this trip?” Some children said yes. Others said no. But now, they all had new memories.
Later, they made posters about nature. Amal's poster said:
Nature is our home.
It gives us water, trees, and air.
Let's take care — it's only fair.
That afternoon, Adam wrote in his notebook: “The planet has so much to see. I have explored a forest, felt sunshine on the beach, and climbed a mountain. Nature is not just green — it is exciting.”

grammar.title: The Present Perfect Tense
grammar.explanation: We use the present perfect to talk about something that happened in the past but still matters now. When you say “I have explored a forest,” it shows this is part of who you are today. It helps you share your experiences with others.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Formula:  subject + have / has + past participle
Use have with I, you, we, they:  “I have explored the coast.”
Use has with he, she, it:  “She has seen the mountain.”
For most verbs, the past participle ends in -ed (explore → explored). Some are irregular (see → seen).
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I have see a bird.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle, not the base verb: “I have seen a bird.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the present perfect as a bridge from the past to today: have / has + the thing you did.
grammar.practice: Write the present perfect. Use have or has + the correct past participle. | I ____ (explore) a cave. | They ____ (see) a shooting star. | She ____ (finish) her nature painting. | We ____ (visit) the forest.

grammar.title: Yes/No Questions with the Present Perfect
grammar.explanation: These questions let us ask about someone's experiences without saying exactly when they happened. They are a great way to start a friendly conversation about nature.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Formula:  Have / Has + subject + past participle + (extra words)?
“Have you ever visited the beach?”
“Has she seen the sunshine in winter?”
The word ever means “at any time in your life”.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Has you ever been to the coast?”  ✗
Fix: use have with “you”: “Have you ever been to the coast?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Have for I, you, we, they. Has for he, she, it. The helping verb jumps to the front to make a question.
grammar.practice: Fill in have or has, and the past participle in brackets. | ______ you ever ____ (go) to a mountain? | ______ she ____ (see) that forest before? | ______ they ____ (explore) the beach yet?

grammar.title: Short Answers
grammar.explanation: Short answers are quick, simple replies. Instead of repeating the whole question, we answer in a few words. This makes talking smooth and natural.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the same helping verb from the question, plus the subject.
“Have you explored a forest?”  →  “Yes, I have.” / “No, I haven't.”
“Has she seen the mountain?”  →  “Yes, she has.” / “No, she hasn't.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “Yes, I have explored a forest.” (too long) or “Yes, I.” (no helping verb)  ✗
Fix: just say the helping verb: “Yes, I have.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember the “A” rule: Always include the Auxiliary (helping) verb — have or has.
grammar.practice: Write a short answer for each question. (Choose Yes or No.) | “Have you seen the sea?”  →  ______, I ______. | “Has your friend explored a cave?”  →  ______, he ______. | “Have they climbed the mountain?”  →  ______, they ______.

grammar.title: Irregular Past Participles
grammar.explanation: Most verbs add -ed for the past participle, but some special verbs do not. These are irregular. We use them a lot with have and has, so it helps to learn them by heart.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
There is no single rule — you memorise them:
see → seen
be → been
freeze → frozen
go → gone    ·    eat → eaten    ·    write → written
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I have saw that lake.”  ✗
Fix: use the past participle: “I have seen that lake.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Make flashcards: the base verb on one side, the past participle on the other. Test yourself every day.
grammar.practice: Fill in with: seen, been, explored, frozen. | She has ____ to the mountains with her family. | The river has ____ over because of the cold. | I have ____ that big forest before. | They have ____ many caves on their trip.

grammar.title: Imperatives (Giving Instructions)
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are sentences that tell someone to do something. They are commands or requests, like “Protect the planet.” They help us give clear instructions.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the base verb (no “to”):  “Explore nature carefully.”
No subject needed — “you” is understood:  “Wear a hat.”
To say “do not”, start with Don't:  “Don't drop litter.”
Add please to be polite, or Let's to suggest doing it together.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You to close the door.” or “To explore the forest.”  ✗
Fix: just the base verb: “Close the door.” / “Explore the forest.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: An imperative is like a coach shouting instructions: verb first, straight to the point!
grammar.practice: Write the imperative using the verb in brackets. | ____ (protect) the planet. | ____ (not drop) litter in the forest. | ____ (listen) to your teacher. | ____ (take) your water bottle on the trip.

grammar.title: Descriptive Phrases + Wh- Questions + Quantifiers (How Much / How Many) + Reported Instructions
grammar.explanation: Descriptive phrases add detail so the reader can picture things clearly. Instead of just “rock”, we can say “a metal rock”. This makes your writing more interesting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Pick a noun:  rock.
Add an adjective that describes it:  metal.
Put them together:  “a metal rock on the beach.”
You can use two adjectives with a comma:  “a large, shiny rock.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “a small gray cat” (two adjectives, no comma) or no adjective at all.  ✗
Fix: add a describing word, and use a comma between two: “a small, gray cat.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Descriptive words “add colour” to your sentence — the more you add, the brighter the picture.
grammar.practice: Add a describing word to make each phrase more interesting. | a ____ mountain | the ____ weather | a ____ beach | Now rewrite this sentence with a descriptive phrase: “The bird sings.” | Lesson 7:  Wh- Questions | What it means and why it matters | Wh- questions start with who, what, where, when, why, or how. They help us ask for exact information and learn about the world. | How to form it | Choose the right wh- word: who (people), what (things), where (places), when (time), why (reasons), how (way). | Add a verb (is, are, do…):  “Where is the coast?” | Finish the question:  “What is the weather like today?” | A common mistake | Mistake: “Where the coast?” (no verb)  ✗ | Fix: add the verb: “Where is the coast?”  ✓ | Memory tip | Say the rhyme: “Who, What, Where, When, Why, and How — ask them well, and you'll learn how!” | Practice 7 | Fill in the correct wh- word. | ____ is the tallest mountain you know? | ____ is the weather like today? | ____ do you go to enjoy nature? | ____ is Earth special? (give a reason) | Lesson 8:  Quantifiers (How Much / How Many) | What it means and why it matters | Quantifiers tell us the amount of something. They make your sentences clear, so people know how much or how many you mean. | How to form it | For things you can count (mountains, trees), use many, a few, several:  “Some mountains are very high.” | For things you cannot count (sunshine, water), use much, a little, a lot of:  “A lot of sunshine is good for plants.” | Some and a lot of can be used for both. | A common mistake | Mistake: “Much children play outside.”  ✗ | Fix: children can be counted, so use many: “Many children play outside.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Count it? Use many. Can't count it? Use much. “A lot of” is a friendly word for both. | Practice 8 | Choose the correct quantifier. | There are ____ (many / much) trees in the forest. | We need ____ (some / much) water for the trip. | She saw ____ (a few / a little) birds on the coast. | There is ____ (a little / many) sand on my shoes. | Lesson 9:  Reported Instructions | What it means and why it matters | Reported instructions tell someone what another person asked us to do. If the teacher says “Bring water,” you can tell your friend: “The teacher said to bring water.” | How to form it | Use said to or told (someone) to + the base verb:  “She told us to wear warm clothes.” | For a “don't”, use not to:  “He said not to run.” | Change the words for “me”, “us”, “my” so the sentence still makes sense. | A common mistake | Mistake: “He said explore the forest.” (no “to”)  ✗ | Fix: add “to”: “He said to explore the forest.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Think of “said to” as a bridge that carries the instruction from one person to another. Never forget the “to”. | Practice 9 | Rewrite as a reported instruction. | “The teacher said, ‘Read the book.’”  →  The teacher said ____ the book. | “Dad told me, ‘Don't forget your hat!’”  →  Dad told me ____ my hat. | “She said, ‘Protect the planet.’”  →  She said ____ the planet.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe a Place in Nature
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say three sentences about a place in nature:
“I like the ______ (beach / forest / mountain).”
“It is ______ and ______.” (describing words)
“There I can see ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the place? Did you use two describing words? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe a place in nature and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Talk About the Weather
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
“Today the weather is ______.”
“The climate where I live is usually ______.”
“My favourite weather is ______ because ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise talk about the weather and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Have You Ever…?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Ask a question: “Have you ever explored a ______ (forest / beach / mountain)?”
The other person answers with a short answer: “Yes, I have!” or “No, I haven't.”
If they say yes, ask: “What did you see?” Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise have you ever…? and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe the Nature Near You
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three or four sentences about the weather and nature near your home or village. Try to use these words: weather, sunshine, and one place word (beach, forest, or mountain).
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about describe the nature near you.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Three Things You Have Done
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about things you have done in nature, using the present perfect:
“I have seen ______.”
“I have explored ______.”
“I have visited ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about three things you have done.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Your Own Nature Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write your own version of “Nature Is Our Home”. Change the middle lines to the parts of nature you like:
Nature is our home, / It gives us ______ and ______, / The ______, the ______, the ______ — / Let's take care, and share it all.
Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about your own nature poem.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (Nature Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
A forest is a big area with many trees.
The coast is the land next to the sea.
Metal is a soft material that you can eat.
Earth is a planet.
Answers: 1. True   2. True   3. False (metal is a hard material used to make things)   4. True
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Match the Word to the Meaning
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each word to its meaning.
climate     a) light and warmth from the sun
sunshine    b) the usual weather in a place
explore     c) to go and look at new places
temperature d) how hot or cold something is
Answers: 1–b   2–a   3–c   4–d
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Fill in the Blanks (have / has)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
I ______ explored a forest.
She ______ seen the mountain.
We ______ visited the beach.
He ______ climbed a hill.
Answers: 1. have   2. has   3. have   4. has
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Care for the Planet
activities.instructionsAndItems: Write two imperatives (instructions) that tell people how to care for nature. Start each one with a verb, for example: “Plant a tree.” Check your ideas with your AI tutor.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Nature and the Environment learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — Describe the Nature Near You and a recording of Speaking 1 — Describe a Place in Nature. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'climate' mean?
quizzes.options: How hot or cold something is. | The light and warmth that come from the sun. | The usual weather in a place over a long time. | Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze)
quizzes.correctAnswer: The usual weather in a place over a long time.
quizzes.explanation: The desert has a hot, dry climate.

quizzes.question: What does 'weather' mean?
quizzes.options: A very tall hill with steep sides. | The light and warmth that come from the sun. | Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze) | What the sky and air are like today.
quizzes.correctAnswer: What the sky and air are like today.
quizzes.explanation: Today the weather is sunny and warm.

quizzes.question: What does 'temperature' mean?
quizzes.options: A big area with many trees. | A very tall hill with steep sides. | How hot or cold something is. | Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze)
quizzes.correctAnswer: How hot or cold something is.

quizzes.question: What does 'sunshine' mean?
quizzes.options: A big area with many trees. | A very tall hill with steep sides. | Land with sand near the sea. | The light and warmth that come from the sun.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The light and warmth that come from the sun.
quizzes.explanation: The sunshine helped the flowers grow.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Coast to Forest': “The _____ is different here — more shade, and less wind.” They walked along a path between the tall trees.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “The climate is different here — more shade, and less wind.” They walked along a path between the tall trees.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Coast to Forest': The sunshine was bright, and the _____ was hot.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The sunshine was bright, and the weather was hot.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Coast to Forest': “What is the _____?” asked the teacher.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “What is the temperature?” asked the teacher.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Coast to Forest': “I wrote about the _____,” said Adam.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “I wrote about the sunshine,” said Adam.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'From Coast to Forest': “But once, water here _____ during a very cold night.” “Frozen water, here in Africa?” asked Amal, surprised.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “But once, water here froze during a very cold night.” “Frozen water, here in Africa?” asked Amal, surprised.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Our Wonderful Nature': High above, the _____ touches the clouds.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: High above, the mountain touches the clouds.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the nature poem with me and explain it?”
“Can you play a ‘Have you ever…?’ listening game with me?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise describing a place in nature?”
“Can you play ‘Have you ever…?’ with me?”
“How do I pronounce ‘temperature’ and ‘climate’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check if my present-perfect sentences are correct?”
“Can you help me make my nature poem rhyme?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on the weather words?”
“What is the difference between weather and climate?”
“Can you help me say ‘temperature’ correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe a beach or a mountain?”
“What is the difference between the coast and the beach?”
“Can you play a nature guessing game with me?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you give me more examples of matter?”
“What are some ways to save energy at home?”
“Can you check my sentence about our planet?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on describing words for nature?”
“Can you help me make my sentences more colourful?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Why do we say ‘she has’ but ‘I have’?”
“Can you give me five more present-perfect questions about nature?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: The usual weather in a place over a long time. The desert has a hot, dry climate.
What the sky and air are like today. Today the weather is sunny and warm.
How hot or cold something is. The temperature is 30 degrees today.
The light and warmth that come from the sun. The sunshine helped the flowers grow.
Turned to ice because it was very cold. (past of freeze) The water in the pond froze in the cold night.
A very tall hill with steep sides. We saw white snow on top of the mountain.
A big area with many trees. The deer ran through the green forest.
Land with sand near the sea. I built sandcastles on the beach.
The land next to the sea. Fishermen live in a village near the coast.
The land, water, animals, and plants around us. We took a walk to enjoy nature.
